perturbation.original_text: Recipient must notify Discloser in writing immediately upon the occurrence of any such unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware and will assist Discloser in remedying the unauthorized use or disclosure of Discloser’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will assist Discloser in remedying the unauthorized use or disclosure of Discloser’s Confidential Information. Recipient must notify Discloser in writing immediately upon the occurrence of any such unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.explanation: Reordering the sentence subtly reduces the emphasis on the immediate notification requirement. This could be argued to weaken the obligation to promptly report breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 45 CFR § 164.410 - Mitigation and data breach notification
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a general NDA, the change diminishes the emphasis on immediate notification. 45 CFR § 164.410 highlights the importance of swift action to mitigate damages from a data breach, a standard expected for companies working with HIPAA-protected entities. Delaying the notification requirement could be seen as a contradiction, especially if the confidential information includes protected health information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Breach Notification Rule | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The reordering of the sentences diminishes the emphasis on immediate notification, potentially contradicting HIPAA regulations that emphasize swift action in data breach scenarios. While both sentences are present, prioritizing assistance over immediate notification weakens the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from HHS.gov, states that the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§ 164.400-414) requires covered entities to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. The reordered text could be interpreted as delaying this notification, which is a violation of the rule's intent, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement will be and will remain the property of the Discloser; all such information in tangible form will be returned to Discloser promptly upon written request or upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and will not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient or its Affiliates or any of their employees.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement will be and will remain the property of the Discloser; all such information in tangible form will be returned to Discloser upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and will not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient or its Affiliates or any of their employees. all such information in tangible form will be returned to Discloser promptly upon written request
perturbation.explanation: Separating the phrase 'promptly upon written request' from the main clause regarding the return of tangible confidential information weakens its enforceability. It creates ambiguity as to when the return is required—only on termination/expiration, or also upon request?
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA § 2(b) allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the return clause ambiguous, it becomes harder to demonstrate a clear violation and obtain an injunction for return of the information. The ambiguity directly impacts the ability to enforce trade secret protections as intended by UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states that confidential information in tangible form will be returned to the Discloser 'promptly upon written request or upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement.' The changed text separates 'promptly upon written request' creating ambiguity. The Law Explanation states that UTSA § 2(b) allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, and by making the return clause ambiguous, it becomes harder to demonstrate a clear violation and obtain an injunction. Although neither of the scraped snippets directly refers to this law, the Law explanation asserts that the change impacts the ability to enforce trade secret protections, which, alongside the Perturbation Explanation, suggests a weakening of a legally relevant obligation. Therefore, the ambiguity created by the structural change contradicts the intent to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will not use any Confidential Information except to the extent necessary for the internal purpose of discussing, analyzing, and exchanging information about each party’s products, services and other offerings to determine whether the parties may enter into a mutually beneficial business relationship (“Purpose”). Recipient will not disseminate or disclose any Confidential Information to any person, firm, business or governmental agency or department, except as such disclosure is expressly permitted in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will not use any Confidential Information except to the extent necessary for the internal purpose of discussing, analyzing, and exchanging information about each party’s products, services and other offerings to determine whether the parties may enter into a mutually beneficial business relationship (“Purpose”). Recipient will not disseminate or disclose any Confidential Information to any person, firm, business or governmental agency or department.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception clause 'except as such disclosure is expressly permitted in this Agreement', this clause becomes overly broad, and any clause allowing disclosure in the agreement contradicts with this restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996, 18 U.S. Code § 1832
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1832) concerns the theft of trade secrets. This change makes a clause overly broad and contradictory by omitting the important exception for allowable disclosure. A reasonable reading would be that absolutely no one can have this information, ever, which would be a contradiction to the act that allows for 'lawful' disclosures. For example, the next clause, about disclosing to employees with a need to know, contradicts this new change.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an exception that allowed for specific disclosures of confidential information permitted within the agreement. The removal of the clause 'except as such disclosure is expressly permitted in this Agreement' creates a contradiction because it now presents an absolute bar on disseminating or disclosing confidential information to any person, firm, business, or governmental agency or department, even if another clause allows such disclosure. This internal contradiction within the contract is a structural flaw making the contract more ambiguous and difficult to enforce. Although the law snippet refers to the 'Theft of trade secrets' it implies there are allowed uses or disclosures and the removal of the exception creates an issue of legal contradiction in the changed text.
